speaker: interviewer
text: Arton. The internet is taking Plainview, New York, United States of America. The interviewer is Ann Bernard, and the interview is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our wedding day which was taken in 1951, in front of-- as we were leaving for the ceremony, out of my-- Renee's home. That's my wife, Renee. This is my youngest son bar mitzvah, Matthew's, and it was taken in front of home here, as you can see. On the left is Stephen, my son Matthew, my wife, Renee, me, and my son, Michael. And that was taken in 1973. This is a picture of my two grandchildren who could not be here today. They live in Churchill, Pennsylvania. These are Steven's two children, from left is Dana, Jordan, myself, my wife, Renee, and her older brother, Eric Jordan. They live in Churchill, Pennsylvania, and they could not be here today. That was taken at Dana's bat mitzvah just recently. This picture is a picture of my entire family. It's me, my grandson Eric, my son Matthew, my grandson Steven, my daughter-in-law Leigh, Dana, then Katie, my son Michael, and my wife Renee. And this was taken in Michael's house. No, that was taken in Pennsylvania. This is a picture of me, of my late son Stephen, and his two children, Dana and Eric. And it was taken at a kiddie ride in Brooklyn. And the reason we went back there-- and that was taken in 1986-- because I used to take my children, and Steven remembered going to those kiddie rides. And when he came in, he wanted to go back, to bring his children there. And that perhaps may be the last picture that I've taken with my late son, Steven. This is a picture taken at a Holocaust observance. That's me, and the county executive Tom [? Galetta ?] presenting a citation to me for all the work that I've done on behalf of the Holocaust Educational Center of Nassau County. This is a picture of-- approximately taken in late 80s-- '88, '89. I had the privilege of bringing the prime minister, then, of Israel Shamir to Nassau. And it was-- the gathering for the entire Jewish community was at Nassau Community College. And background is the Secret Service people. And we walked them through the-- some of the lockers that you can see, for safety reasons. He was very impressed that day, because he had said to me when he arrived-- we've met him in Queens with a complete police escort, motorcycles, police cars, and even mounted police. And there was a beautiful setting. When he came out of his limo, he said to me he never gives a greeting like this in Israel. So he was very pleased to come. Those were the words he said-- he said, nobody welcomes me like this in Israel. This is my citizenship papers. It was taken-- I was made a citizen in Brooklyn in 1952, in February. I had long awaited-- I wanted to become a citizen. It was something that I strived to. And as you can see, I made it to the six years. You had to wait six years before you could become a citizen, so this is my citizenship paper that I'm officially an American citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: The date is February 26, 1952. This is a document of a purchase of the home that my dad had purchased for us in Podkamien. And the year is 1927. That was an official document, like you would say here when you go to closing. It was the closing day, and taking possession of the home that we had in Podkamien. This is a prayer book which was-- belonged to the [INAUDIBLE], the second rabbi that lived in our town. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. And the reason for me to tell-- I carried this with me when-- throughout all the years. He had given it to me, and I just held on to it. And the reason for me to turn to this page is, as you read here, it says [SPEAKING HEBREW] It was because of, when we were marched to work, and-- there was always one fella that used to lead us in that song. And even today, when I-- I tremble when I go to shul. And [INAUDIBLE] is only said on holidays. The meaning is, open the gate and let the righteous enter. And as he started that song, you could hear 800 responding. [SPEAKING HEBREW] This is the will of God. And it's sad, when you come back and think about the march and the song. The Bible itself is probably-- my estimate is 80 years old, as you can see by the pages. This is a Megillat Esther that belonged to my grandfather. He always said that he wanted me to have it, because I-- at the age of 13, even though he was gone, it was the first time that I read the megillah out of his megillah. And it's 100 years old. It tells a story of the Jews, but I think it's time for a second megillah, with the Holocaust. It's probably a much more greater megillah story that we survivors have to tell, than the Megillah of Esther. So I've been holding on to it, and I will probably donate it to the Holocaust Educational Center so they can show it and display it there, and perhaps even, if any other-- put it on loan, because it is over 100 years old, that megillah. And that almost concludes my story today. This is my family. On my left is my son Matthew. On my lap, I got my favorite grandson, Steven. Next to me is my wife Renee of 45 years. And on her lap is my favorite granddaughter, Kate. And on Renee's right is my sweet daughter-in-law Leigh. And back of Leigh, from my left-- from my right to life is my son Michael, which I'm very proud of my children and grandchildren. And next to Michael is Eileen-- because I can't see, but I hope I'm right. And Neil-- Neil and Eileen are very dear friend of ours. As I did in my video, I told you how I came to meet Eileen, under the circumstances when we going to shul in [INAUDIBLE]. We became very friendly. And they're very involved in the Holocaust Educational Center. Neil is treasurer, Eileen is our executive director. But both of them, they're very, very dear friends. Very caring and concerned two people. And sometimes you go through traveling, and you do a lot of volunteerism. But it's rewarding when you meet friends like this that you make. So these are very close people to Renee and me-- and the kids, everybody. Very concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just go back for a few minutes-- we talked about your bar mitzvah. I wonder if you could just make a few comments about that, which we had not included in the testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: My bar mitzvah took place in 1939, around the late fall, which would take place on [INAUDIBLE]. It's just before Hanukkah. And-- in fact, it could have been shabbos Hannukah. The most important thing at my bar mitzvah was that it took place on a Thursday. So you made sure that you put your tefillin on-- can't do it on a shabbot. But on shabbot, I was assigned to read the whole Torah portion. And it was a happy day, even though it was already the war years 1939. Of course, catering was limited, but it was a good kiddush-- that, I can still remember- at my mom's house. And everybody came from shul, and celebrated. And my grandparents, which lived seven kilometers away from us, came in for shabbos. And everybody was there, so it was a joyous occasion. But the family that I recall had had probably the last joyous occasion in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: My bar mitzvah was a little more a joyous occasion. And, you know-- years have gone by since then. You know, we've been blessed to have many more joyous occasions. We've also had our share of tragedy. I'm sure my father spoke about my brother Steven, who was killed about 10 years ago. Not here with us are his children, Eric and Dana. But they're good kids, and we love them all. But the truth be told, my father, growing up-- or, as I remember growing up, did not speak very much about his experiences during the Holocaust I have heard bits and pieces over the years-- some from him, but more so from my grandmother, who told us the story about how they were hidden in the-- in the barn, by the Polish farmer, with all the members of the family. I'm glad that he is giving his testimony, because it's important to speak about what he went through, and what his family went through, as well as Jews went through in Europe, during the 30s and the 40s. I also know that he speaks to groups today about his experiences. And I think that's important, as well, because we're at the point where many survivors are getting older. And without putting it onto tape and preserving it, soon they'll be nobody to speak firsthand about what happened. And it's important that we never forget what happened, so that we don't allow it to happen again. I'm proud of the fact that he is doing it. I'm proud of the fact that he's involved, and he's continually been involved in all kinds of different Jewish organizations. And we're just very, very, very proud of him. We love him very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to add anything on top of that. But I know that we're all very proud of daddy's accomplishments, and thankful for the support that-- that mom has given him all these years. I-- I-- I-- I guess I'm proud of two things-- one, that he's made it a point to relay the message of-- of surviving the Holocaust to the community. And that, at the same time, gotten himself to the point where he can tell his story for his own family. And I'm really, really proud of that. When-- when God saved-- saved him from the Holocaust, he saved us all. And I'm just really proud that he's my dad. NEIL (FRIEND): I think one's outlook on life, their personality, their character is formed by-- by many events. One of which is having one's life saved some years ago had to produce a character and a personality in Boris that-- that kind of explains the-- the community service that he gives of himself. And that's-- he also gives of himself to many people. I've never seen him turn anybody down who was in need of something, who needed some help. He'll do what he can for a lot of people. When he came to Eileen and I, and asked if we would get involved in the Holocaust Center of Nassau County to help teach the children about the atrocities that happened, we were very honored to be part of the center. And we're very proud to be friends with Boris and his lovely family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. It's been a pleasure to be here today with you, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] I would just like to add something-- that I'm very proud to be his wife, to live with him, to know him. As he said, 45 years, we've had tragedies, but we've also had good times. And if it wasn't for him, we wouldn't have all these wonderful people with us-- my children, my grandchildren, my daughter-in-law, and my two grandchildren who couldn't be with us, and my son, who couldn't be with us. We miss him. And he would be very proud of his dad, as we all are today. I just hope that he's going to live to be strong enough and keep working toward the goals that he set out to do. And I thank you for being with us today. And that's all I have to say.
